_meta.description: MH370 parsed dataset from public sources
sources: Inmarsat Signalling Unit Log (CNN PDF release, May 27 2014)
ATSB MH370 Definition of Underwater Search Areas (June 2014)
DSTG Bayesian Methods paper (Holland et al, 2017)
Independent Group analyses (Iannello, Godfrey, Ulich et al)
warnings: BFO values at 18:25:27 and 18:25:34 are flagged UNRELIABLE - SDU warm-up transient after power cycle
BTO at 18:25 is debated - some analysts believe the aircraft position implied is inconsistent with radar track
BFO values are NOT raw signal measurements - they are derived values after Inmarsat internal processing
Timestamps have millisecond precision in PDF but native resolution unknown (may be float or integer microseconds)
All BFO values carry unknown vertical velocity component that is uncompensated by aircraft SDU
Military radar data (17:22-18:22 UTC) has never been independently verified by a technical body
Gap between 17:07 and 18:25 has NO Inmarsat data - ACARS off, SDU powered down

satellite.note: Satellite has a ±0.6 deg north-south oscillation over 24h period. BFO calculations must account for this drift. Early analysis by Inmarsat FAILED to correct for this - later revised.

known_positions.note: Last confirmed ACARS position. After this, ACARS disabled.
Passed waypoint IGARI. Last civilian radar contact. Handed to Ho Chi Minh - never checked in.
Transponder goes to standby. Disappears from civilian radar.
Military radar picks up unidentified return. Aircraft turning back westward.
Aircraft near VAMPI waypoint, north of Penang. Flying northwest up Malacca Strait.
Last confirmed radar return. Near waypoint MEKAR, Andaman Sea. After this - nothing confirmed.
known_positions.reliability: FLAGGED - never independently verified. Thailand military also had radar coverage but reported late.

inmarsat_handshakes.note: Pre-departure calibration. Aircraft at gate. Known position. Use for BTO bias calibration.
Shortly after takeoff. BTO implies ~2305 NM from satellite - consistent with KUL position.
Last ACARS. BFO only - no BTO available for this message type. Position from ACARS: 5.27N 102.79E

SDU reboot after power cycle. BFO unreliable due to oscillator warm-up transient. This is the controversial ping - some analysts say BFO here implies northward heading, others southward. BTO implies aircraft ~1570 NM from satellite.

7 seconds after reboot request. BFO still in warm-up transient. +130 Hz change in 7s is anomalous - no plausible maneuver explains this without warm-up transient. BTO here is inconsistent with 18:25:27 value - implies aircraft moved ~130 NM in 7 seconds, which is impossible. Likely measurement artifact.
First clean data point with both BTO and BFO. BTO ring radius ~4740 NM from satellite. BFO implies aircraft moving away from satellite - consistent with southward heading at cruise speed.
BTO ring radius ~5100 NM. Aircraft moving further from satellite. BFO lower - consistent with heading more perpendicular to satellite direction.
BTO ring radius ~5540 NM. Still moving further from satellite. BFO continuing to decrease.
BTO ring radius ~5990 NM. BFO slight increase - possible minor heading change.

Malaysia Airlines attempted to call aircraft. Triggered a transmission. BTO very similar to arc 5 - aircraft moved very little in 33 minutes. Either speed reduced significantly, or heading changed to more tangential to satellite.
Final arc 6. BTO implies ~6150 NM from satellite. BFO 182 Hz - same as arc 2, suggesting similar speed/heading component toward/away from satellite.

SDU reboot triggered by APU auto-start after engine flameout. BTO identical to arc 6 - aircraft barely moved in 8.5 minutes. BFO here may already be affected by descent/dive. This defines THE 7TH ARC.

8 seconds after 00:19:29. BFO jumped +70 Hz. Official interpretation: aircraft in steep accelerating dive at ~14,800 fpm. Competing interpretation (Declination Model): aircraft descending more gently and turning eastward. This is the LAST TRANSMISSION. No BTO available - logon was never completed.

inmarsat_handshakes.message_type: IFE setup message (EXPECTED but NOT RECEIVED)
inmarsat_handshakes.note: In-Flight Entertainment system should have sent a setup ping ~90 seconds after SDU reboot. It was never received. Four possible explanations: (a) IFE manually switched off from cockpit, (b) IFE/SDU lost power due to APU flameout, (c) APU ran out of fuel before IFE initialized, (d) aircraft already impacted. The absence of this ping is used to argue aircraft hit water before 00:21. But explanation (a) - deliberate pilot action - cannot be ruled out.

bto_to_range_formula.description: Convert BTO microseconds to slant range in km
bto_to_range_formula.note: The fixed offset accounts for processing delays in ground station, satellite, and aircraft terminal. This value is empirically derived from pre-flight known-position pings. If this offset is wrong by even 1 microsecond, the arc radius shifts by ~150 meters.

bfo_model.description: BFO is affected by multiple Doppler components
components: Aircraft horizontal velocity component toward/away from satellite (PARTIALLY compensated by SDU)
Aircraft vertical velocity component (NOT compensated - this is what reveals descent)
Satellite north-south orbital drift (±3 deg/24h oscillation - must be calibrated out)
Aircraft SDU oscillator bias (fixed per unit, ~+150 Hz for 9M-MRO)
SDU warm-up transient after power cycle (affects first 2-3 minutes after reboot)
bfo_model.note: The SDU assumes satellite is perfectly stationary. It is not - this introduces a heading-dependent error that the standard 'Up-Down Model' ignores. The Declination Model attempts to correct for this.

confirmed_debris.note: First confirmed MH370 debris. Barnacle analysis suggests origin in warm tropical water, not cold southern ocean - disputed by drift modelers.
Damage pattern on this flap contradicts flaps-extended ditching hypothesis. ATSB: consistent with high-speed impact, not controlled landing.

searched_zones.note: Used towed sonar vehicles. 2km track spacing in some areas. Some terrain too rough - data holidays exist.
AUV fleet, faster coverage. Extended north and widened the band.
Only completed half of planned 15,000 km2 due to weather. Ocean Infinity stated they believe aircraft is NOT in searched area.

key_anomalies_to_investigate.description: The BTO at 18:25:27 implies aircraft ~1570 NM from satellite. The BTO at 18:25:34 (7 seconds later) implies ~1400 NM. A 170 NM position change in 7 seconds is physically impossible at any aircraft speed. One or both values contain a measurement error or the formula is being misapplied during SDU warm-up.

Inmarsat-3F1 oscillates ±0.6 degrees north-south every 24 hours. At 00:19 UTC the satellite was at approximately 1.6 degrees south of its nominal position. Original analysis did not correctly account for this. Later corrected, but the correction magnitude is debated.

BTO at arc 6 (00:10:58) and arc 7 (00:19:29) are both 18,400 microseconds - identical. In 8.5 minutes at 471 knots cruise speed, the aircraft should have moved ~67 NM and the BTO should have changed. Either the aircraft dramatically slowed/turned, or there is a BTO quantization issue at this value.

There are ZERO Inmarsat records between 17:07 and 18:25 UTC - a 78-minute gap. During this time the aircraft made its U-turn, crossed Malaysia, and flew up the Malacca Strait. Why did the SDU go completely silent? If only ACARS was disabled, the SDU should still have responded to pings. This suggests either full power-down or SDU was deliberately disabled.

The BFO values from arc 2 onward are consistent with BOTH a southward track AND a northward track, depending on assumptions about speed and the satellite drift correction. The northern route was never formally excluded by the BFO data alone - it was excluded by combining BFO with fuel range assumptions.
